nativeRange.habitat: Clear, shaded karst streams and tributaries in Sulawesi (Celebes); surface and sub-surface zone over rocky or sandy substrate with overhanging riparian vegetation; moderate to swift current

decorPreferences: floating plants (water lettuce, frogbit, Amazon frogbit) to diffuse light and provide cover
overhanging or emergent vegetation at the waterline
gentle surface current via spray bar or low-flow filter outlet
driftwood and leaf litter for tannins and visual breaks

diet.notes: In the wild, the Celebes Halfbeak feeds at the water's surface, taking small flying and terrestrial insects, aquatic invertebrates, and zooplankton from the surface film. In captivity it readily accepts small floating live or frozen foods such as fruit flies, crickets, mosquito larvae, bloodworms, brine shrimp, and Daphnia. High-quality floating micro-pellets and freeze-dried insects are generally accepted once the fish is acclimated to captive foods. Sinking foods are largely ignored. Variety in the diet supports long-term health.

goodWith: small peaceful surface or mid-water fish of similar size (rasboras, small danios)
bottom-dwelling species that do not compete for surface zone (corydoras, small loaches)
avoidWith: fin-nippers (tiger barbs, serpae tetras) — vulnerable extended lower beak
large or aggressive fish that may bully surface dwellers
Nomorhamphus ebrardtii and other congeneric halfbeaks (interspecies aggression and hybridisation risk)
very small fish or fry (predation risk)
boisterous fast-swimming species that stress surface-dwelling halfbeaks

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Nomorhamphus liemi; shows adult male with diagnostic mandibular extension.

summary: The Celebes Halfbeak is a viviparous livebearer native to clear karst streams in Sulawesi, Indonesia. Males develop a striking fleshy, red-tipped lower jaw extension that is vulnerable to fin-nipping tankmates. The species is an active jumper and requires a tight-fitting lid, a surface-focused diet, and quiet, similarly sized companions.
careNotes: The Celebes Halfbeak is one of the few truly viviparous halfbeaks — females give birth to fully formed live young rather than laying eggs, an unusual trait within Zenarchopteridae. It is endemic to clear, moderately fast-flowing karst streams in Sulawesi (the Celebes), Indonesia, and is occasionally confused with the closely related N. ebrardtii; the two should not be mixed as they interbreed and show interspecies aggression. Males are smaller and more colourful than females and develop a diagnostic fleshy, red-tipped mandibular extension (the “lower beak”); this structure is fragile and vulnerable to fin-nipping tankmates. A tight-fitting lid is non-negotiable: this species is an active surface jumper and escapes readily. Maintain a well-shaded tank with floating plant cover, gentle surface movement, and soft to moderately hard, slightly acid-to-neutral water (pH 6.8–7.8; gH 5–15) to replicate Sulawesi karst conditions. Temperature stability matters more than the precise value; avoid chilling below 22°C. Keep in groups of at least four (ideally 1 male to 2–3 females) to distribute male attention. Males may show mild intraspecific aggression; heavier planting reduces confrontations. Feeding entirely on surface foods, this species will largely ignore sinking pellets or bottom-placed wafers.
breedingNotes: The Celebes Halfbeak is internally fertilising and viviparous, meaning females give birth to fully formed live young rather than laying eggs. Males transfer sperm using a modified anal fin called the andropodium; females can store sperm and produce successive broods without re-mating. Gestation runs approximately 6–8 weeks depending on temperature, yielding small broods of 5–20 relatively large fry that are born fully formed and immediately free-swimming at the surface. No parental care is shown, and adults will consume fry if given the opportunity. The female should be moved to a separate, densely planted fry tank shortly before birth, or dense floating plant cover should be provided to give fry refuge. Fry accept newly hatched brine shrimp, micro-worms, and finely crushed floating flake from birth. Conditioning adults on live insects such as fruit flies and mosquito larvae prior to a breeding attempt tends to improve brood frequency.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nomorhamphus liemi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to ~6 cm, females to ~9 cm TL), temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.8–7.8, hardness 5–15 dH, native range (Sulawesi, Indonesia), habitat (clear karst streams, surface zone), diet (surface insectivore), breeding notes (viviparous, andropodium, 5–20 fry), and care notes including escape risk, beak fragility, and confusion with N. ebrardtii.

additional.name: FishBase — Nomorhamphus liemi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Zenarchopteridae, Beloniformes), adult size (to 9 cm TL), native range (Sulawesi), habitat, and diet. Confirmed viviparous reproductive mode and surface-dwelling behaviour. Provided basis for adultSizeCm max of 9.0 cm.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Halfbeak Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide for halfbeaks including N. liemi; confirmed minimum tank size (~60 L / 15 gal), escape risk (tight lid requirement), floating plant recommendations, and surface-only feeding behaviour. Noted intermediate care level and preference for peaceful tankmates.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Halfbeaks
additional.notes: Feature article covering Nomorhamphus halfbeaks; highlighted mandibular extension fragility, intraspecific male aggression, viviparity, and the importance of distinguishing N. liemi from N. ebrardtii. Confirmed soft, slightly acid water preference for Sulawesi stream species.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist (TFH) — Halfbeaks in the Aquarium
additional.notes: TFH feature on halfbeak husbandry; described sex differences, the andropodium in Nomorhamphus males, brood size (typically 5–20 fry), gestation period (~6–8 weeks), and fry diet. Supported group-keeping recommendations and escape risk classification.

fish.breedingNotes: The species is viviparous, with internal fertilisation carried out via the male's andropodium (a modified anal fin). Females can store sperm and produce successive broods without re-mating. Gestation lasts approximately 6–8 weeks, after which broods of 5–20 large, free-swimming fry are born at the surface. Adults will eat fry if not separated; the female should be moved to a separate tank near term, or dense floating plant cover provided. Fry accept newly hatched brine shrimp and micro-worms from birth.
